summary: This lesson reviews subjunctive usage with the new verb dejar (to leave a message) across several trigger structures: quiero que, cuando + future, es esencial que. It introduces ojalá (hopefully) as a subjunctive trigger and reviews the relationship between -tial endings in English and -cial in Spanish.

teachings.summary: Recap of subjunctive structures using dejar: after quiero que (desire), after cuando (future reference), after es esencial que (judgment), and after ojalá (hope). The subjunctive conveys desire/order vs the indicative which states fact.
teachings.rule: Quiero que + subjunctive (desire/order). Quiero decirle que + subjunctive = I want to tell him to... vs que + indicative = I want to tell him that he is...

examples.spanish: Quiero que me dejes un mensaje
examples.english: I want you to leave me a message

examples.spanish: Quiero decirle que me deje un mensaje
examples.english: I want to tell him to leave me a message

examples.spanish: Cuando te deje el mensaje, llámame
examples.english: When he leaves you the message, call me

examples.spanish: Es esencial que vea mi mensaje
examples.english: It's essential that he sees my message

teachings.notes: Using indicative after decir que states a fact (he IS leaving a message). Using subjunctive conveys an order/desire (to leave a message).

teachings.summary: Ojalá means 'hopefully' and is followed by the subjunctive. It has Arabic origins related to 'Allah', reflecting 800 years of Muslim influence on Spanish.
teachings.notes: Possibly from 'ojo-Allah' (eye of God). Very common expression. After ojalá, use subjunctive.

teachings.summary: English words ending in -tial become -cial in Spanish, similar to the -tion to -ción pattern.

vocabulary.derivation: Not the same as salir (to leave/go out)

vocabulary.english: I mean / that is to say
vocabulary.derivation: Literally 'or be' — uses subjunctive of ser (sea)
